text: The surgeon establishes pneumoperitoneum and identifies a healthy colonic loop alongside a large parastomal hernia, consistent with findings from the clinical examination.
The surgical field reveals minimal adhesions from previous surgeries, with the surgeon carefully inspecting the area for any significant scar tissue.
The surgeon uses gentle blunt dissection to divide pelvic adhesions between the small bowel loops and the rectal stump, separating these structures in the surgical field.
The surgeon divides minor adhesions in the left lower quadrant and carefully frees the end of the colostomy from the anterolateral abdominal wall.
The surgeon carefully disconnects the colostomy from the surrounding skin edges and fascia, ensuring clear separation of these structures.
The surgeon trims the edges of the colostomy and inserts the anvil of the stapler into the proximal colon, securing it with Proline II-O sutures.
During the disconnection of the colostomy from the skin, a small abscess cavity with a stoma fistula is visible at the superior mucocutaneous border of the colostomy.
The surgeon performs wide debridement of the tissue down to healthy adipose tissue, but the condition of the area prevents the insertion of mesh for hernia repair.
The surgeon repairs the defect following excision of the hernia sac using interrupted non-absorbable sutures, ensuring closure of the area.
The surgeon re-establishes the pneumoperitoneum and mobilizes the left colon up to the splenic flexure, ensuring sufficient length for a tension-free anastomosis.
The surgeon mobilizes the base of the colonic mesentery, ensuring adequate exposure and preparation for the subsequent surgical steps.
The surgeon performs a high dissection to avoid injury to the ureter and colonic vessels, carefully navigating around these structures.
The surgeon carefully isolates the rectal stump from the posterior aspect of the bladder using blunt dissection techniques, avoiding sharp or energy-based tools.
The surgeon inserts a circular stapler through the anus and performs an anastomosis using the Knight-Griffin technique, completing the double stapling process.
The mobilized colon is positioned in the pelvis without any visible tension, indicating sufficient length for the anastomosis.
The surgeon checks the alignment of the colon to ensure it is not twisted before closing the stapler, maintaining proper orientation for the anastomosis.
The surgeon closes the circular stapler and fires it, completing the anastomosis. The stapler is then gently removed through the anus.
A blank drain is placed in the pelvis at the end of the operation, positioned to manage postoperative fluid accumulation.
The surgeon closes the parietal peritoneum using intracorporeal sutures and Protack, securing the tissue layers within the abdominal cavity.
